EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down from estimated wind gusts of 50 knots ( 58 mph ).

An area of low pressure that developed off the mid-Atlantic coast on the morning of the 6th intensified as it moved slowly northeastward through the Gulf of Maine on the 7th.  The storm produced heavy snow across all of western and central Maine with amounts generally ranging from 30 to 40 inches in the western mountains to 10 to 15 inches along the coast.

Training thunderstorms produced very heavy rains across the region...with rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches occurring in a short time. The heavy rains overwhelmed drainage systems causing extensive flooding along Main Street...which was closed to traffic for a time...and water flooded stores along Main Street. Several feet of water were reported in store basements. Flooding was also reported on other roads in the Van Buren vicinity. Culverts and roads were damaged to varying extents.Above normal precipitation occurred across northern Maine during the month of July. The above normal rainfall contributed to saturated soil and unseasonably elevated water levels for area streams and brooks. This allowed smaller streams and brooks to rise rapidly in response to this heavy rain event. The Van Buren area in particular was the focus for heavy rains from this event along with several events in early August. Flash flooding was also reported in the Van Buren area on August 8th and 10th. A FEMA damage survey was conducted in late August which encompassed this event together with the flash flood events which occurred on August 8th and 10th.
A tree and power lines were blown down along Main Street from estimated wind gusts of 50 knots ( 58 mph ).

Low pressure over Illinois on the morning of the 10th moved northeast and was located just north of Lake Huron by the morning of the 11th.  A secondary low formed in southeastern Pennsylvania and moved northeast through Maine to the Gaspe Peninsula by the morning of the 12th.  The secondary low created a a strong, warm,  and very moist southeast flow across the state.  The southeast flow enhanced the precipitation in the mountains where amounts ranged from 2 to almost 3 inches during the event. In addition, temperatures rose to near 50 degrees which caused considerable snow melt.  On Friday morning, the 12th, water rose rapidly between 4:30 and 6:30 am behind a serious ice jam that formed on the Sandy River downstream of the Route 4 bridge in Farmington.  The resulting flood submerged 39 cars in the parking lot of the University of Maine at Farmington.   Another Ice jam formed on the Androscoggin River flooding portions of Route 232 and Route 2 in Rumford.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Mid Atlantic Coast...through the Gulf of Maine...to Nova Scotia brought heavy snow and near blizzard conditions to the region. Snow began during the evening of the 6th then continued...heavy at times...through the 7th before tapering to snow showers during the early morning hours of the 8th. Winds of 20 to 30 mph combined with the heavy snow to produce near blizzard conditions at times during the height of the storm. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches at lower elevation areas. Persistent upsloping winds into the mountains enhanced accumulations across higher terrain areas where total snow accumulations of 20 to 35 inches were reported.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Mid Atlantic Coast...through the Gulf of Maine...to Nova Scotia brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the region. Snow began during the afternoon of the 6th...with blizzard conditions developing that night.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph were common at the height of the storm. The strong winds and heavy snow frequently reduced visibilities below a quarter mile along with producing extensive blowing and drifting snow. Although blizzard conditions diminished during the morning of the 7th...snow continued through the rest of the 7th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches...with localized totals to around 16 inches.

Intensifying low pressure tracking along the Maine coast to central Nova Scotia produced heavy snow across the region. The snow started during the early morning hours of the 15th. Very heavy snow fell during the 15th with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times...particularly across northern portions of the region. Strong winds produced near blizzard conditions at times with extensive blowing and drifting snow along with visibilities reduced below a quarter mile at times. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches along the Downeast coast...with localized totals to around 18 inches. Across interior Downeast areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches...with localized totals to around 24 inches. Across central and northern areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 16 to 24 inches with localized totals to around 30 inches. The 27.3 inch storm total at Caribou ( 27.1 inches of which fell during the 15th ) represented the second greatest snowfall ever recorded.

Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking through the Saint Lawrence River Valley produced strong damaging south to southeast winds across the region. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common...with gusts in excess of 60 mph occurring along the coast with onshore winds. A peak wind gust to 62 mph was reported at Stonington. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees which fell on powerlines contributing to power outages. Several thousand customers lost power at various times. The winds also damaged roofs and signs.

The combination of a rapidly melting snowpack and 2.50 to 3.50 inches of rain caused flooding along the Piscataquis River at Dover - Foxcroft. The river crested at 11.1 feet...just above the flood stage of 11.0 ft. Several buildings near the river were evacuated...with water reported in the basements of several buildings. Snow that had been plowed into culverts and ditches disrupted the normal drainage of water. This produced standing and running water on several roads. The running water undercut and eroded several roads requiring road closures.

Strong onshore winds occurred in advance of intensifying low pressure tracking along the Maine coast.  Wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph occurred for several hours. A peak wind gust to 74 mph was reported at West Quoddy. The strong onshore winds had warmed temperatures enough so that precipitation had transitioned to mostly rain during the time of strong winds. The strongest wind gusts occurred during the morning hours.

Across interior Downeast areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches...with localized totals to around 24 inches. Across central and northern areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 16 to 24 inches with localized totals to around 30 inches. The 27.3 inch storm total at Caribou ( 27.1 inches of which fell during the 15th ) represented the second greatest snowfall ever recorded.

Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking through the Saint Lawrence River Valley produced strong damaging south to southeast winds across the region. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common with gusts in excess of 60 mph occurring along the coast with onshore winds. A peak wind gust to 73 mph was reported at Jonesport. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees which fell on powerlines contributing to power outages. Hundreds of customers lost power at various times. The winds also damaged roofs and signs.

Intensifying low pressure tracking along the Maine coast to central Nova Scotia produced heavy snow across the region. The snow started during the early morning hours of the 15th. Very heavy snow fell during the 15th with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times...particularly across northern portions of the region. Strong winds produced near blizzard conditions at times with extensive blowing and drifting snow along with visibilities reduced below a quarter mile at times. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches along the Downeast coast...with localized totals to around 18 inches. Across interior Downeast areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches...with localized totals to around 24 inches. Across central and northern areas...storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 16 to 24 inches with localized totals to around 30 inches. The 27.3 inch storm total at Caribou ( 27.1 inches of which fell during the 15th ) represented the second greatest snowfall ever recorded.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Mid Atlantic Coast...through the Gulf of Maine...to Nova Scotia brought heavy snow and near blizzard conditions to the region. Snow began during the evening of the 6th then continued...heavy at times...through the 7th before tapering to snow showers during the early morning hours of the 8th. Winds of 20 to 30 mph combined with the heavy snow to produce near blizzard conditions at times during the height of the storm. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 20 inches at lower elevation areas. Persistent upsloping winds into the mountains enhanced accumulations across higher terrain areas where total snow accumulations of 20 to 35 inches were reported.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Mid Atlantic Coast...through the Gulf of Maine...to Nova Scotia brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the region. Snow began during the afternoon of the 6th...with blizzard conditions developing that night.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph were common at the height of the storm. The strong winds and heavy snow frequently reduced visibilities below a quarter mile along with producing extensive blowing and drifting snow. Although blizzard conditions diminished during the morning of the 7th...snow continued through the rest of the 7th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches...with localized totals to around 16 inches.

A strong low pressure system moving northeast from northern Maine to the Canadian Maritimes brought high winds to western and southern Maine.  The high winds downed trees onto power lines causing widespread power outages.  At the peak of the event early Friday morning, power outages affected 45,000 customers in more than 175 communities.  Downed trees also temporarily blocked several roads, and caused property damage to buildings and vehicles.

Intense low pressure tracking through the Saint Lawrence River Valley produced strong winds across the region. The storm produced sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph...with gusts of 45 to 55 mph common. A gust of 59 mph was reported at Portage. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees which fell on power lines...contributing to power outages. Approximately 15,000 customers lost power across the region  at various times during the storm. The strong winds also damaged several roofs. A building under construction was blown over in Mapleton.

Above normal precipitation occurred across northern Maine during July and early August. The above normal rainfall contributed to saturated soils and unseasonably elevated water levels for area streams and brooks. Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rains which overwhelmed drainage systems causing flooding along Main Street. A hospital located along Main street received water damage.A FEMA damage survey was conducted in late August which encompassed this event and several events in the Van Buren area extending from early August back through late July.

Above normal precipitation occurred across northern Maine during July and early August. The above normal rainfall contributed to saturated soils and unseasonably elevated water levels for area streams and brooks. Heavy rains from slow moving thunderstorms caused rapid rises along the Little Madawaska River...in the vicinity of Westmanland...which then overflowed and flooded surrounding areas. Water depths of up to 3 feet were reported on portions of the Little Madawaska Road...with the flood waters also threatening a campground.

Above normal precipitation occurred across northern Maine during July and early August. The above normal rainfall contributed to saturated soils and unseasonably elevated water levels for area streams and brooks. Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rains...with 2 to 3 inches of rain falling in a short time...which overwhelmed drainage systems causing flooding along some streets and allowing water to flood several stores. Several small streams and brooks also overflowed their banks. This was the second flash flood event to affect Van Buren in just over a week.A FEMA damage survey was conducted in late August which encompassed this event...another flash flood event on August 10th along with the flash flood event from July 30th. The survey also included a flash flood event which occurred in Fort Kent on August 8th.

Above normal precipitation occurred across northern Maine during July and early August. The above normal rainfall contributed to saturated soils and unseasonably elevated water levels for area streams and brooks.Streams and brooks rose in response to around 2 inches of rain which fell across the region over several hours. The Violette Creek overflowed...flooding the basements of 6 homes located near the creek and damaging the St. Marys Road which runs along the creek. Several other roads were also closed due to flooding.  This was the third flash flood event to affect the Van Buren area in less than 2 weeks.Flooding from this event and the several previous events severely eroded several streams banks and washed out portions of several roads causing extensive damage in spots.A FEMA damage survey was conducted in late August which encompassed this event...the flash flood event on August 8th and also the flash flood event on July 30th. The survey also included a flash flood event which occurred in Fort Kent on August 8th.
Trees and power lines were blown from estimated wind gusts of 50 knots ( 58mph ).
A 75 foot tall pine tree 18 inches in diameter was snapped near the base from estimated wind gusts of 56 knots ( 65 mph ) near Plaisted Lake.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley produced very strong winds across the region.Winds increased quickly during the morning...then continued through the afternoon. Around 18,000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time across northern and eastern Maine. So many customers lost power across the state of Maine that the governor declared a State of Emeregency.With the leaves still on many trees and the wet soil...the winds were able to snap branches and topple trees. Many of the branches and trees knocked down power lines contributing to numerous power outages.

Trees and power lines were blown down from estimated wind gusts of 56 knots ( 65 mph ). One tree landed on the back of a car stopped at an intersection...the driver was not injured. A large section of wooden roof was torn from a business...which landed on a tractor trailer parked near the building.
Lightning struck the chimney of a home in Windham and ignited a fire that badly damaged the second floor of the structure.
A man working outside a building in Eliot was injured when lightning struck nearby.  He was taken to a local hospital as a precautionary measure and later released.
A severe thunderstorm moving through York County knocked over trees in Acton/Newfield area.
A severe thunderstorm moving through Cumberland County knocked over trees in Pownal.
A severe thunderstorm moving through Sagadahoc County knocked over trees in Bowdoinham.

The strongest winds generally occurred near the coast with onshore winds. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees which brought down power lines and contributed to power outages. Nearly 5000 customers...mostly near the coast...lost power at various times during the storm.

Intensifying low pressure tracking from the Great Lakes through the Saint Lawrence River Valley produced very strong winds across the region.Winds increased quickly during the morning...then continued through the afternoon. Around 18,000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time across northern and eastern Maine. So many people lost power across the state of Maine that the governor declared a State of Emergency.With the leaves still on many trees and the wet soil...the winds were able to snap branches and topple trees. Many of the branches and trees knocked down power lines contributing to numerous power outages. Along the coast...some boats were torn from their moorings and ran aground or were swamped due to the strong winds and rough seas. At Bar Harbor...a nearly 110 foot excursion vessel broke from its moorings and ran aground on a near island.

Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking along the New England coast...through the Gulf of Maine to the maritimes...brought general snow accumulations of 8 to 14 inches. However...localized accumulations to around 18 inches were also reported. Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph produced considerable blowing and drifting snow...along with significantly reduced visibilities.

Low pressure developed off the Virginia coast on the morning of the 3rd and intensified as it moved slowly northeastward through the Gulf of Maine on the 4th.  The storm brought heavy snow to all of mid-coast and western Maine with 10 to 20 inches reported from the coast to the foothills and generally 7 to 11 inches reported in the mountains.  Strong northeasterly winds accompanied the storm causing a storm surge which combined with high astronomical tides to cause coastal flooding along the coast of York and Cumberland Counties.  Residents along Middle Beach and Great Hill Road were evacuated around 11 am.  Rocks the size of bread boxes covered the road up to 3 feet deep.  The seawall was ruptured at Gooches Beach allowing the tide to tear up sidewalks and fences.  In Wells, a portion of the city's seawall was damaged and debris covered Webhannet Drive.  In Saco, several feet of sand piled up on the streets of Camp Ellis and the tide took out jersey barriers on Eastern Avenue.  In Portland, the tide gauge was above flood stage for almost 2 hours from 1045 am until 1243 pm.  The peak gusts reported at the Portland Weather Buoy was 41 kts while the Matinicus Rock Buoy reported a peak gust of 63 kts.

An area of low pressure moved across New England on the 20th, then intensified rapidly on the 21st and 22nd over the Canadian Maritimes as a large area of high pressure moved from western Canada into the north-central United States.  The area of high pressure continued to move south into the southern United States through the 24th.  The pressure gradient between the low over the  Maritimes and the area of high pressure produced west to northwest winds across the area and brought cold temperatures into the region.    The cold temperatures and west to northwest winds combined to produce an extended period of cold wind chill temperatures.  Wind chill temperatures during the period generally ranged from 15 to 30 degrees below zero.

The peak gusts reported at the Portland Weather Buoy was 41 kts while the Matinicus Rock Buoy reported a peak gust of 63 kts.

An area of low pressure moved across New England on the 20th, then intensified rapidly on the 21st and 22nd over the Canadian Maritimes as a large area of high pressure moved from western Canada into the north-central United States.  The area of high pressure continued to move south into the southern United States through the 24th.  The pressure gradient between the low over the  Maritimes and the area of high pressure produced west to northwest winds across the area and brought cold temperatures into the region.    The cold temperatures and west to northwest winds combined to produce an extended period of cold wind chill temperatures.  Wind chill temperatures during the period generally ranged from 15 to 30 degrees below zero.

Low pressure developed off the Virginia coast on the morning of the 3rd and intensified as it moved slowly northeastward through the Gulf of Maine on the 4th.  The storm brought heavy snow to all of mid-coast and western Maine with 10 to 20 inches reported from the coast to the foothills and generally 7 to 11 inches reported in the mountains.  Strong northeasterly winds accompanied the storm causing a storm surge which combined with high astronomical tides to cause coastal flooding along the coast of York and Cumberland Counties.  Residents along Middle Beach and Great Hill Road were evacuated around 11 am.  Rocks the size of bread boxes covered the road up to 3 feet deep.  The seawall was ruptured at Gooches Beach allowing the tide to tear up sidewalks and fences.  In Wells, a portion of the city's seawall was damaged and debris covered Webhannet Drive.  In Saco, several feet of sand piled up on the streets of Camp Ellis and the tide took out jersey barriers on Eastern Avenue.  In Portland, the tide gauge was above flood stage for almost 2 hours from 1045 am until 1243 pm.  The peak gusts reported at the Portland Weather Buoy was 41 kts while the Matinicus Rock Buoy reported a peak gust of 63 kts.

An area of low pressure that moved northeastward from the lower Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 22nd to the Mid-Atlantic Coast by the morning of the 23rd, brought a mixture of precipitation to the state.  Most areas saw the precipitation change from snow to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain.  Thunderstorms were also reported in scattered locations throughout the state.  Some coastal locations saw the precipitation change to rain as temperatures warmed above freezing.  Although snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches, the combination of strong winds on the back side of the storm and ice-coated tree limbs caused branches to fall onto power lines and created scattered power outages.  Winds gusted to around 40 mph across the area.  More than 12,000 customers lost power across southern and central Maine.

Arctic air was drawn across the region on strong winds during the 14th. Low temperatures across northern Maine on the 15th generally ranged from 20 to 30 below zero...although local readings to around 40 below zero were reported. Low temperatures across Downeast areas on the 15th generaly ranged from 15 to 25  below zero...with localized temperatures around 30 below zero. Wind chills of 45 to 55 below zero were common across northern areas...with locally colder readings. Wind chills Downeast generally ranged from 35 to 45 below zero. Temperatures remained below zero across northern areas and struggled to reach the single digits above zero Downeast on both the 14th and 15th. Low temperatures and wind chills across the region on the 16th were similar to those of the 15th. This represented one of the coldest arctic outbreaks to affect the region in several years.

An area of low pressure deepened rapidly as it moved slowly northeastward from the Carolina Coast on the morning of the 1st to south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 2nd.  The storm brought 6 to 10 inches of snow to the mountains and foothills.  Along the coast and in the interior, the snow mixed with or changed to sleet, freezing rain, or rain which kept the accumulations lower.  Interior sections generally received 4 to 8 inches while the coast received only 2 to 6 inches.

Most areas saw the precipitation change from snow to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain.  Thunderstorms were also reported in scattered locations throughout the state.  Some coastal locations saw the precipitation change to rain as temperatures warmed above freezing.  Although snowfall amounts generally ranged from 1 to 2 inches, the combination of strong winds on the back side of the storm and ice-coated tree limbs caused branches to fall onto power lines and created scattered power outages.  Winds gusted to around 40 mph across the area.  More than 12,000 customers lost power across southern and central Maine.

An area of low pressure deepened rapidly as it moved slowly northeastward from the Carolina Coast on the morning of the 1st to south of Nova Scotia by the morning of the 2nd.  The storm brought 6 to 10 inches of snow to the mountains and foothills.  Along the coast and in the interior, the snow mixed with or changed to sleet, freezing rain, or rain which kept the accumulations lower.  Interior sections generally received 4 to 8 inches while the coast received only 2 to 6 inches.

Low pressure intensifying late on the 27th over the Canadian Maritimes brought a continuation of cold temperatures along with northwest winds to the area, and caused a brief period of cold wind chill temperatures overnight before the winds diminished. Rapidly intensifying low pressure slowly tracked through the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from Sunday the 2nd through the early morning hours of Monday the 3rd. The storm brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the region. Heavy snow developed during the morning of the 2nd and continued into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Localized snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were reported at times from the evening of the 2nd through the early morning hours of the 3rd before the snow gradually diminished in intensity before ending. Strong winds developed during the afternoon and evening of the 2nd with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and frequent gusts to around 45 mph. Similar wind speeds then persisted through mid morning of the 3rd. Even though the intensity of the snowfall diminished early on the 3rd...the strong winds and remaining snow still produced blizzard conditions and frequent whiteouts through mid morning of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 14 inches across northwest portions of the region to 12 to 18 inches across eastern areas.

Arctic air was drawn across the region on strong winds during the 14th. Low temperatures across northern Maine on the 15th generally ranged from 20 to 30 below zero...although local readings to around 40 below zero were reported. Low temperatures across Downeast areas on the 15th generaly ranged from 15 to 25  below zero...with localized temperatures around 30 below zero. Wind chills of 45 to 55 below zero were common across northern areas...with locally colder readings. Wind chills Downeast generally ranged from 35 to 45 below zero. Temperatures remained below zero across northern areas and struggled to reach the single digits above zero Downeast on both the 14th and 15th. Low temperatures and wind chills across the region on the 16th were similar to those of the 15th. This represented one of the coldest arctic outbreaks to affect the region in several years.

The storm brought heavy snow...sleet and freezing rain to the region.Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches...although localized totals up to 18 inches were reported across northern portions of Washington county. Sleet and freezing rain also accompanied the storm with ice accumulations of generally 1/4 to 3/4 of an inch...although localized ice accumulations of around an inch were reported.

Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph...with gusts to 45 mph...combined with recent snow to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow. The snow blowing from high snow banks along area roads produced extensive drifting across the roads with frequent whiteout conditions. An area of low pressure moved across New England on the 20th, then intensified rapidly on the 21st and 22nd over the Canadian Maritimes as a large area of high pressure moved from western Canada into the north-central United States.  The area of high pressure continued to move south into the southern United States through the 24th.  The pressure gradient between the low over the  Maritimes and the area of high pressure produced west to northwest winds across the area and brought cold temperatures into the region.    The cold temperatures and west to northwest winds combined to produce an extended period of cold wind chill temperatures.  Wind chill temperatures during the period generally ranged from 15 to 30 degrees below zero.

Rapidly intensifying low pressure slowly tracked through the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia from Sunday the 2nd through the early morning hours of Monday the 3rd. The storm brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the region. Heavy snow developed during the morning of the 2nd and continued into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Localized snowfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour were reported at times from the evening of the 2nd through the early morning hours of the 3rd before the snow gradually diminished in intensity before ending. Strong winds developed during the late morning and afternoon of the 2nd with sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph and frequent gusts to around 45 mph. Similar wind speeds then persisted through mid morning of the 3rd. Even though the intensity of the snowfall diminished early on the 3rd...the strong winds and remaining snow still produced blizzard conditions and frequent whiteouts through mid morning of the 3rd.Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 14 inches across northwest areas...with 18 to 24 inches of snow common across northeast areas. This was one of the most significant storms to affect northeast Maine in nearly 20 years and the 23.8 inches of snow reported at Caribou represented the second greatest snow event ever. Snow drifts in excess of 8 feet were reported across northeast areas.

Wind from a large dust devil in the town of Lebanon reportedly lifted the roof of a two-story body shop causing it to collapse.  A 51-year-old man was inside the shop at the time and was killed in the incident.

An ice jam on the Aroostook river near the village of Crouseville...just southeast of the town of Washburn...caused an increase in river levels. The flood stage at Washburn is 14 feet and the river level briefly crested at just over 14 feet...before rapidly falling back below flood stage. Minor flooding of a boat launch was observed at Washburn. Ice was forced onto a dirt road bordering the river near the town of Wade. In the village of Crouseville...river ice damaged a pasture fence bordering the river and water innundated one residential backyard.

Severe thunderstorms moving through western Maine produced damaging winds and hail in parts of the state.  In York County, winds brought down wires and/or poles in Acton, Shapleigh, and Alfred.  In Rumford, the winds downed trees, caused a hanger at Dyer Airport to collapse, and damaged several planes.  Lightning from the storms ignited several small fires in the area around Rumford.  Trees were also reported down in the Wilton/Industry area.

Severe thunderstorms moved through Kennebec and Waldo Counties between 2 and 5 pm.  The storms produced a wind gust of 45 kts at the Waterville Airport and downed trees in Freedom and Searsmont. A slow moving cold front became stalled over New England on the 26th resulting in over 3 inches of rain on already saturated ground.  This front finally moved offshore on the 28th.  Another low developed over the southeastern states on the morning of the 28th, then moved northeast through New England on the 29th.  Another 1 to 3 inches of rain fell across the area causing rivers to flood.  At Skowhegan, the Kennebec River was above the 35,000 cfs flood flow from 10:00 pm on the 29th to 11:30 pm on the 30th, reaching a maximum flow of 50,500 cfs at 8:00 am on the 30th.  The Kennebec River in North Sidney was above the 17 foot flood stage from 9:30 am on the 30th to 4:30 pm on the 30th (crest of 17.4 feet at 1:30 pm on the 30th).  At Augusta, the Kennebec River was above the 13 foot flood stage from 3:15 pm on the 30th to 8:15 pm on the 30th (crest of 13.4 feet at 6:15 pm on the 30th).

A strong low pressure system moving northeast along the Saint Lawrence River Valley caused damaging winds over western and southern Maine.  The high winds downed trees and limbs causing property damage and numerous power outages.  A number of buildings and vehicles were damaged by falling trees.  A number of boats broke loose from their moorings and either sank or were beached.  A partially constructed roof collapsed on a 43 year-old male causing serious injury.  Power outages affected 70,630 customers.  Statewide, about 20% of the population was affected by power outages, prompting the Governor to declare a state of emergency.  Some areas were without electric power for 3 days.  Peak wind gusts were measured at 62 mph at Cape Elizabeth and 53 mph in Bath and Rockland.

Low pressure developed over north-central North Carolina on the morning of the 17th and moved rapidly northeastward.  The low intensified during the day and passed to the west of the state overnight.   The  low was accompanied by a deep flow of moisture from the  Gulf of Mexico. Dewpoints in eastern North Carolina were in the mid 60s to the east of the low.  A strong south to southeast wind developed across the state which enhanced the amount of precipitation that fell in the mountains.  The southeast flow also caused temperatures to rise into the mid 40s at many locations.  Precipitation amounts were heaviest in the mountains where over 3 inches of rain fell in places.  An observer in Andover recorded 3.41 inches of precipitation.  Many homes and roads were flooded. Ice jams also played a part in the severity of the flooding.  At one point the town of Bethel was completely cutoff as all roads in and out of the town were closed due to ice jams.  A trailer Park in West Bethel was evacuated because the water had reached 4 to 5 feet deep.  The towns in Maine that were most affected were Bethel, Rumford and Canton.  In Canton the local school and fire station were flooded, as well as about 50 homes.  Approximately 120 residents of a nursing home were also evacuated.  In Farmington, Route 4, the main thoroughfare, and Front Street were flooded and several businesses were inundated.  Many roads across western Maine were washed out as a result of the combination of high rainfall rates and rapid snow melt.  Some other towns affected by flooding were Solon, Bingham, North Anson, Anson, Dixfield, Milton, North Woodstock, and Roxbury.  Franklin, Kennebec, Oxford, Somerset and Waldo Counties received a presidential disaster declaration due to the flooding. The rainfall and snowmelt also caused the Carrabassett, Kennebec, and Androscoggin Rivers to flood.  The Kennebec River at Skowhegan was above the 35,000 CFS flood flow from 10:30 am on the 18th to 1:00 am on the 20th, and crested at 78,100 CFS at 7:30 pm on the 18th.  At North Sidney, the Kennebec River was above the 17 ft flood stage from 4:30 pm on the 18th to 11:15 pm on the 19th, and crested at 22.21 feet at 12:15 am on the 19th.  At Augusta, the Kennebec River was above the 13 ft flood stage from 5:15 pm on the 18th to 4:15 am on the 20th, and crested at 18.39 feet at 2:00 am on the 19th.The Carrabassett River at North Anson was above the 15 ft flood stage from 7:30 am to 3:30 pm on the 18th, and crested at 16.58 feet at 10:45 am.The Androscoggin River at Auburn was above the 13 ft flood stage from 10 pm on the 18th to 1:15 am on the 20th, and crested at 14.18 feet at 5:15 pm on the 18th.

In Canton the local school and fire station were flooded, as well as about 50 homes.  Approximately 120 residents of a nursing home were also evacuated.  In Farmington, Route 4, the main thoroughfare, and Front Street were flooded and several businesses were inundated.  Many roads across western Maine were washed out as a result of the combination of high rainfall rates and rapid snow melt.  Some other towns affected by flooding were Solon, Bingham, North Anson, Anson, Dixfield, Milton, North Woodstock, and Roxbury.  Franklin, Kennebec, Oxford, Somerset and Waldo Counties received a presidential disaster declaration due to the flooding. The rainfall and snowmelt also caused the Carrabassett, Kennebec, and Androscoggin Rivers to flood.  The Kennebec River at Skowhegan was above the 35,000 CFS flood flow from 10:30 am on the 18th to 1:00 am on the 20th, and crested at 78,100 CFS at 7:30 pm on the 18th.  At North Sidney, the Kennebec River was above the 17 ft flood stage from 4:30 pm on the 18th to 11:15 pm on the 19th, and crested at 22.21 feet at 12:15 am on the 19th.  At Augusta, the Kennebec River was above the 13 ft flood stage from 5:15 pm on the 18th to 4:15 am on the 20th, and crested at 18.39 feet at 2:00 am on the 19th.The Carrabassett River at North Anson was above the 15 ft flood stage from 7:30 am to 3:30 pm on the 18th, and crested at 16.58 feet at 10:45 am.The Androscoggin River at Auburn was above the 13 ft flood stage from 10 pm on the 18th to 1:15 am on the 20th, and crested at 14.18 feet at 5:15 pm on the 18th.
